votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the modular exponentiation logic using pow(2, n, p) and handles the implication structure of the specification correctly.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, using pow(2, n, p) to compute (2^n) mod p.

The Python implementation of the specification and precondition correctly matches the Coq logic, using Python's built-in modular exponentiation function pow(base, exp, mod) which is equivalent to the Coq expression (2^n) mod p for non-negative n and positive p.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The precondition checks n >= 0 and p > 0. The spec checks that output equals 2^n mod p when the precondition holds, and returns True (vacuously satisfied) otherwise. Python's pow(2, n, p) computes 2^n mod p efficiently, which matches Coq's (2 ^ n) mod p for all concrete inputs satisfying the precondition.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. problem_49_pre checks n >= 0 and p > 0. problem_49_spec returns True when the precondition fails (matching Coq's implication), and when the precondition holds, it checks output == pow(2, n, p), which computes 2^n mod p, matching Coq's (2 ^ n) mod p.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. problem_49_pre checks n >= 0 and p > 0. problem_49_spec returns True when the precondition fails (matching Coq's implication), and when the precondition holds, it checks output == pow(2, n, p), which computes 2^n mod p, matching Coq's (2 ^ n) mod p.
Matches the Coq spec: when n>=0 and p>0 it checks output == (2**n) mod p via pow(2,n,p); otherwise returns True (vacuous implication). Modular arithmetic and exponent semantics align.
Both require n>=0 and p>0; when true, Python uses pow(2,n,p) which equals (2^n) mod p. If the precondition is false, both specs are vacuously true.
Precondition matches and, when satisfied, Python uses pow(2, n, p) which equals (2^n) mod p for n>=0 and p>0; otherwise the spec is vacuously true.
